articles.title: Press Release by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus regarding the UN
articles.summary: With regard to the United Nations (UN) Secretary-General’s Mission of Good Offices and UNFICYP Reports to the UN Security Council, the Ministry of Foreign
articles.content_html:
With regard to the United Nations (UN) Secretary-General’s Mission of Good Offices and UNFICYP Reports to the UN Security Council, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus notes the following:

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus welcomes the Secretary-General’s expressed commitment to continue his efforts to resume the negotiation process, with the aim of achieving a viable and comprehensive solution, on the basis of the agreed framework and the relevant UN Security Council Resolutions. We share the Secretary-General’s view that the confidence building measures cannot replace the negotiation process. We look forward to the resumption of the negotiations the soonest, aiming to achieve an enduring, comprehensive and just settlement of the Cyprus issue, based on a bicommunal bizonal federation with political equality, as set out in the relevant UN Security Council Resolutions,

With regard to the Report on UNFICYP, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus shares the concern of the Secretary-General regarding the recorded ongoing violations by the occupying army in the buffer zone, especially in the areas of Pyla, Varosha and Strovilia. We unequivocally condemn the illegal actions of the Turkish occupying army, including the harassment of Cypriot citizens and the restrictions it tries to impose on UNFICYP’s movements and its ability to implement its mandate.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus respects and attaches great importance to UNFICYP’s role in Cyprus, based on its mandate, as set out by UN Security Council Resolution 186(1984), and expects UNFICYP to undertake all necessary measures for the immediate reversal of the recorded violations and the safeguarding of normal living conditions.

At the same time, however, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus expresses its disappointment with the longstanding tendency of the UN to maintain an equidistant approach, resulting to the inaccurate and selective depiction of the realities on the ground as these are caused by the Turkish occupation and the methodology followed by the occupying forces, undermining the effort for the creation of a climate of trust.

Regarding the humanitarian mandate of the Peacekeeping Force, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus wishes to recall the need for the Report to clearly record and highlight Türkiye’s responsibilities both in relation to the living conditions of the enclaved, as well as, on the issue of missing persons.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus reaffirms its commitment to continued cooperation and close coordination with UNFICYP for the effective implementation of its mandate, and with the ultimate goal of a just and viable solution to the Cyprus issue, in accordance with the relevant UN Security Council Resolutions.

(AP/KA/GS)

articles.title: Ανακοίνωση του Υπουργείου Εξωτερικών αναφορικά με τη δήλωση του Υπουργείου Εξωτερικών της Τουρκίας για το
articles.summary: Το Υπουργείο Εξωτερικών της Κυπριακής Δημοκρατίας απορρίπτει και καταδικάζει τους ισχυρισμούς που περιλαμβάνονται στη δήλωση του Υπουργείου Εξωτερικών της
articles.content_html:
Το Υπουργείο Εξωτερικών της Κυπριακής Δημοκρατίας απορρίπτει και καταδικάζει τους ισχυρισμούς που περιλαμβάνονται στη δήλωση του Υπουργείου Εξωτερικών της Τουρκίας αναφορικά με το Ψήφισμα που υιοθέτησε το Ευρωπαϊκό Κοινοβούλιο στις 8 Ιουλίου 2026.

Το Ευρωπαϊκό Κοινοβούλιο καταδίκασε, για ακόμη μία φορά και με τον πλέον κατηγορηματικό τρόπο, τη στρατιωτική εισβολή της Τουρκίας και τη συνεχιζόμενη παράνομη κατοχή μέρους του εδάφους της Κυπριακής Δημοκρατίας, ως σοβαρή παραβίαση του διεθνούς δικαίου και ως εμπόδιο στην ειρήνη, τη σταθερότητα και τις σχέσεις Ευρωπαϊκής Ένωσης-Τουρκίας.

Καταδίκασε, περαιτέρω, στη βάση των θεμελιωδών αξιών και αρχών της Ευρωπαϊκής Ένωσης, τις σοβαρές παραβιάσεις ανθρωπίνων δικαιωμάτων που απορρέουν από την τουρκική εισβολή, με ειδική αναφορά στη σεξουαλική βία κατά γυναικών και κοριτσιών κατά τη διάρκεια και μετά την εισβολή του 1974.

Η Τουρκία υπενθυμίζεται ότι εξακολουθεί να φέρει την ευθύνη, βάσει του διεθνούς δικαίου, για τις εν λόγω παραβιάσεις και καλείται να αναγνωρίσει τις αποτρόπαιες αυτές πράξεις, να αναλάβει τις ευθύνες της και να παράσχει πλήρεις και αποτελεσματικές επανορθώσεις.

Πενήντα δύο χρόνια μετά την τουρκική εισβολή και τη συνεχιζόμενη στρατιωτική κατοχή, η Τουρκία εξακολουθεί να υπέχει ευθύνη έναντι της διεθνούς κοινότητας, καθώς και έναντι των θυμάτων και των επιζώντων, των οποίων ο πόνος και η αντοχή αξίζουν μνήμη, δικαιοσύνη και σεβασμό.

(ΑΦ/ΕΑθ/ΑΣ)

articles.title: Statement by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus regarding the illegal ‘‘memorandum of
articles.summary: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus condemns the signing of the illegal ‘‘memorandum of understanding’’ between Türkiye and the
articles.content_html:
The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus condemns the signing of the illegal ‘‘memorandum of understanding’’ between Türkiye and the secessionist entity in the occupied areas of the Republic of Cyprus, for the development of an undersea natural gas pipeline which will connect Türkiye with the occupied areas. The “memorandum” in question, which was signed during the illegal visit of the Vice President of Türkiye, Mr Cevdet Yılmaz, to the occupied areas of the Republic of Cyprus, constitutes yet another manifestation of Türkiye’s revisionist policy in the Eastern Mediterranean and is part of the ongoing efforts to consolidate faits accompli of the occupation and the further integration of the occupied areas with Türkiye.

Under International Law, including the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), the unilateral development of energy infrastructure and the promotion of projects of strategic importance, both on the territory and in the maritime zones of the Republic of Cyprus, without the permission of the Republic of Cyprus, constitutes a violation of its sovereignty and sovereign rights. Türkiye, as the occupying power, bears full responsibility for the activities taking place in the occupied areas of the Republic of Cyprus.

The Republic of Cyprus denounces Türkiye’s latest illegal activities and will continue to do so in international fora, demanding that Türkiye finally demonstrates respect for International Law, including UNCLOS, as well as the relevant United Nations Security Council Resolutions.

The Republic of Cyprus will continue to defend its legitimate rights, intensifying its efforts at a legal, political and diplomatic level, on the basis of International and European Union Law, in all competent Organisations, including the European Union.

(AP/GS/ΚΑ)

articles.title: Welcoming Address by the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Dr Constantinos Kombos, at the High-Level Conference on Coexistence and Interreligious Diversity, 26 June 2026
articles.summary: Religious faith is a deeply personal matter. Yet, it shapes societies, defines regions, directs the future, while at the same time coming from the past. It is
articles.content_html:
Religious faith is a deeply personal matter. Yet, it shapes societies, defines regions, directs the future, while at the same time coming from the past. It is indeed a most powerful thing. And consequently, it is much easier to contrast, to isolate, and to focus on those that share your religious standpoint.

To echo the arguments of those that are in agreement with you, to look at the world through that specific personal prism. However, it is more valuable to meet and discuss with those that seek peaceful coexistence, by acting outside the comfort zone. Peace is, after all, not found in comfort zones. Your presence here today attests to this. The Ministry of Foreign Affairs is therefore extremely proud to host this kind of a conference on coexistence and inter-religious diversity. It is an attempt to bridge religion, tolerance, dialogue and regional peace. Moreover, we wanted to project the merits of such an event, at a moment where it will have the maximum impact.

This is why today’s event is taking place under the auspices of the Cyprus Presidency of the Council of the European Union (EU).

I would also like to express our sincere appreciation to the King Hamad Global Centre for Peaceful Coexistence for co-hosting this conference. We have discussed this idea well before the war, back in Bahrain almost a year ago, and since then, the principles promoted by the Centre have become even more important. It is therefore a great pleasure to have as partner in this his Excellency, Sheikh Abdulla bin Ahmed Al Khalifa. Thank you, my friend.

Ladies and gentlemen,

Cyprus stands at the crossroads of Europe, the Middle East and North Africa. It is a bridge for culture, civilisations, and inter-religious tolerance.

Cyprus carries an important heritage of the three monotheistic faiths. This is our history, our reality and our future. This shapes how we see our role in the region as a participator in dialogue, and, where we can, as a facilitator for co-existence.

These days we are witnessing growing polarisation. We see conflict, displacement. We see the rise of radicalism. Or more accurately, we sense that it is brewing. We see the ancient communities in the region under pressure. We see anger and confrontation driving policy.

In such times, the need for dialogue is greater than ever and co-existence is part of the answer. Not as a matter of choice, but as an underlying necessity. It is important to come to the realisation that co-existence cannot be taken for granted. It must be safeguarded. It must be strengthened with urgency because the danger is growing.

Dialogue is inherently linked to the task of countering that threat. As is religious diversity. After all, religious freedom is not just an abstract principle. It is rather a fundamental condition for peaceful and resilient societies. Therefore, co-existence, diversity and religious freedoms are the integral ingredients.

It is for this reason that the Republic of Cyprus has placed inter-religious dialogue and the protection of religious communities among the priorities of our Presidency. To this end, we prepared the ground by the President starting with the appointment in December 2024 of a Special Representative for Religious Freedom and the Protection of Minorities in the Middle East.

We have since raised funds and established contacts with actors sharing our clear objectives. The objectives to promote sustainable dialogue and to coordinate support on co-existence. To bring communities within the region closer. By using financial tools, by raising awareness, providing platforms to the experts, by promoting good practices and identifying new products. These are the goals that guide us and have driven the organisation of this Conference. A Conference that brings together religious leaders, policymakers, academia and representatives of civil society.

Dear friends,

Cyprus acts again as a meeting point for dialogue, with emphasis on understanding that we share more than could divide us. This is not, after all, a competition between identities. It is part of this region’s special identity, and it can contribute to peacebuilding. Because religious leadership can serve as agency for reconciliation. Because institutions can work to better safeguard the tolerance that is vital to pluralistic societies. Because experts are the answer to the distorted, simplistic and dividing narrative.

In conclusion, I want to assure you that the Republic of Cyprus will continue to serve as a platform for dialogue. We carry the messages of this Conference to the EU, that is today present. A strong, clear voice in Cyprus. Your work, your commitment today, your findings, are going to guide us in our future steps.

So, I want to thank you for being here, and I wish you every success in your deliberations.

Thank you.

(AP/MS/AS)

articles.title: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus and the King Hamad Global Center for Coexistence
articles.summary: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus and the King Hamad Global Center for Coexistence and Tolerance of the Kingdom of Bahrain co-hosted a
articles.content_html:
The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus and the King Hamad Global Center for Coexistence and Tolerance of the Kingdom of Bahrain co-hosted a High-Level Conference on Coexistence and Interreligious Diversity in Lefkosia on 25–26 June 2026, in the framework of the Cyprus Presidency of the Council of the European Union (EU).

The Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, inaugurated the Conference and delivered opening remarks together with the Minister of Transportation and Telecommunications of the Kingdom of Bahrain and Chairman of the Board of Trustees of the King Hamad Global Center for Coexistence and Tolerance, Dr Shaikh Abdulla bin Ahmed Al Khalifa.

As Minister Kombos highlighted, Cyprus aims to serve as a platform for interfaith dialogue across the region, including among the three monotheistic faiths, while reinforcing Cyprus’ role as a bridge between the EU and the broader Middle East.

The EU Commissioner for Internal Affairs and Migration, Mr Magnus Brunner, and the EU Commissioner for the Mediterranean, Ms Dubravka Šuica, also addressed the Conference, while the EU Special Envoy for the Promotion of Freedom of Religion or Belief outside the EU, Ms Mairead McGuinness, delivered the keynote speech.

The partnership with King Hamad Global Center for Coexistence and Tolerance, an institution renowned globally for its niche contribution in promoting religious freedom, coexistence and tolerance, is of high value in this regard. The co-organisers further agreed to mutually develop a Joint Cyprus–Bahrain Roadmap on Coexistence, outlining priority areas for cooperation and knowledge exchange.

By bringing together voices from Europe and the Gulf, the Conference establishes Cyprus and Bahrain as partners in advancing cross-regional dialogue on coexistence, while contributing to stronger Europe-Gulf cooperation in an increasingly interconnected world.

The Conference was organised under the auspices of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus and led by the Special Representative for Religious Freedoms and the Protection of Minorities in the Middle East, Ambassador Thessalia Salina Shambos.

The conference gathered policy-makers, academics, researchers and representatives of civil society and local communities from Cyprus, Bahrain, the EU, the Gulf region and beyond, to exchange perspectives and strengthen cooperation.

(AP/KA/GS)

articles.title: Statement by the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, following
articles.summary: Dear Minister, dear Ferit, let me start by thanking you for visiting Cyprus. I want to welcome you and your delegation.
articles.content_html:
Dear Minister, dear Ferit, let me start by thanking you for visiting Cyprus. I want to welcome you and your delegation.

This is the second visit of the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Albania to Cyprus within a year, and I visited Tirana back in September, so there is considerable movement and mobility at the political level.

It is also important to note that this year marks the 35th anniversary since the establishment of diplomatic relations between our two countries, and we have witnessed through these contacts that there is a growing and intensifying political momentum trying to push the bilateral relation forward.

Today, we had the opportunity to reflect and assess the progress made, but also to try and map out how we can move to the future. We confirmed, of course, our shared commitment in relation to deepening the bilateral relations. We agreed that we need to intensify dialogue, political dialogue and technical dialogue at all possible levels. We reviewed the institutional framework, which is solid, but there is always, we agree, room for further improvement.

We also focused on significant aspects of sectors upon which we can build and try and explore untapped potential, like tourism, investment, services, digital transformation, education, and we have tried to set out a pathway, a road map of moving ahead with meetings of the Chambers of Commerce, of the business and investment communities in the forthcoming period.

In addition, of course, today we discussed Albania’s European path in accession negotiations. Back when I visited last September, we made a promise as a forthcoming Presidency that we will be an honest broker and facilitator of the remarkable effort of the reforms that have been carried out by Albania and its Government, and I think we have done everything in our power, in our role as Presidency of the Council to deliver on exactly those promises. This of course relates to the whole enlargement process, especially in relation to the Western Balkans, and more specifically to Albania. We would like to take this opportunity to congratulate you, Minister, for the significant progress that the European Union (EU) has documented and has responded to in the last few months.

Today, I firmly believe that Albania stands closer than ever to its European future. There is some work to be done, but rest assured that, in that process, Cyprus will remain an honest facilitator.

In addition to that, we had today the opportunity to go through some regional matters, and some of those we will continue the discussion about later on.

Relating to the Western Balkans, we briefly touched upon the issue. This is an area of priority for the EU. It is a geopolitical, strategical goal of the EU, and the accession process is directly connected and linked to that priority. The enlargement process must take place in a way that is creating a positive environment for both the EU and the countries involved. So, in general, we encourage the reform agendas of those countries to proceed, and we want to make sure that the EU will offer the necessary reciprocity, something that has not been always clearly the case in the past.

Finally, as regards the Cyprus issue, I briefed my colleague about the latest developments, about how things could move forward, about the role of the EU in all this. And, of course, I reiterated our commitment to the resumption of meaningful negotiations, to dialogue, always and exclusively within the framework set by the United Nations (UN) Security Council and what the EU framework also provides for and dictates. And I want to take the opportunity to thank Albania for its principled stance and its European positioning as regards the Cyprus issue.

In conclusion, dear Minister, dear Ferit, it was a great pleasure having you and your delegation here in Lefkosia and Cyprus, and I look forward to the continuation of our deliberations.

Thank you.

(AP/MS/IA)
articles.content_text:
Dear Minister, dear Ferit, let me start by thanking you for visiting Cyprus. I want to welcome you and your delegation.
This is the second visit of the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Albania to Cyprus within a year, and I visited Tirana back in September, so there is considerable movement and mobility at the political level.
It is also important to note that this year marks the 35th anniversary since the establishment of diplomatic relations between our two countries, and we have witnessed through these contacts that there is a growing and intensifying political momentum trying to push the bilateral relation forward.
Today, we had the opportunity to reflect and assess the progress made, but also to try and map out how we can move to the future. We confirmed, of course, our shared commitment in relation to deepening the bilateral relations. We agreed that we need to intensify dialogue, political dialogue and technical dialogue at all possible levels. We reviewed the institutional framework, which is solid, but there is always, we agree, room for further improvement.
We also focused on significant aspects of sectors upon which we can build and try and explore untapped potential, like tourism, investment, services, digital transformation, education, and we have tried to set out a pathway, a road map of moving ahead with meetings of the Chambers of Commerce, of the business and investment communities in the forthcoming period.
In addition, of course, today we discussed Albania’s European path in accession negotiations. Back when I visited last September, we made a promise as a forthcoming Presidency that we will be an honest broker and facilitator of the remarkable effort of the reforms that have been carried out by Albania and its Government, and I think we have done everything in our power, in our role as Presidency of the Council to deliver on exactly those promises. This of course relates to the whole enlargement process, especially in relation to the Western Balkans, and more specifically to Albania. We would like to take this opportunity to congratulate you, Minister, for the significant progress that the European Union (EU) has documented and has responded to in the last few months.
Today, I firmly believe that Albania stands closer than ever to its European future. There is some work to be done, but rest assured that, in that process, Cyprus will remain an honest facilitator.
In addition to that, we had today the opportunity to go through some regional matters, and some of those we will continue the discussion about later on.
Relating to the Western Balkans, we briefly touched upon the issue. This is an area of priority for the EU. It is a geopolitical, strategical goal of the EU, and the accession process is directly connected and linked to that priority. The enlargement process must take place in a way that is creating a positive environment for both the EU and the countries involved. So, in general, we encourage the reform agendas of those countries to proceed, and we want to make sure that the EU will offer the necessary reciprocity, something that has not been always clearly the case in the past.
Finally, as regards the Cyprus issue, I briefed my colleague about the latest developments, about how things could move forward, about the role of the EU in all this. And, of course, I reiterated our commitment to the resumption of meaningful negotiations, to dialogue, always and exclusively within the framework set by the United Nations (UN) Security Council and what the EU framework also provides for and dictates. And I want to take the opportunity to thank Albania for its principled stance and its European positioning as regards the Cyprus issue.
In conclusion, dear Minister, dear Ferit, it was a great pleasure having you and your delegation here in Lefkosia and Cyprus, and I look forward to the continuation of our deliberations.
Thank you.
(AP/MS/IA)

articles.title: Press Release of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus on the decision of the Committee
articles.summary: The Committee of Deputy Ministers of the Council of Europe (Human Rights), examined Türkiye’s compliance with the ECtHR’s judgment on property cluster in the
articles.content_html:
The Committee of Deputy Ministers of the Council of Europe (Human Rights), examined Türkiye’s compliance with the ECtHR’s judgment on property cluster in the context of the 4th interstate Case Cyprus v. Türkiye. Türkiye failed to convince the Deputy Ministers about its compliance with the judgment.

The Turkish proposal failed to receive the necessary majority.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus expresses its satisfaction regarding today’s decision. In spite of the increasing trend of the Turkish position for closure throughout the years, thanks to the systematic and targeted diplomatic efforts of the Republic of Cyprus, in close coordination with the Law Office of the Republic and with the contribution of the Deputy Minister for European Affairs, succeeded to block the closure of the cluster and simultaneously to persuade for the need of referring an interpretative question back to the ECtHR in regards to the necessary measured required for Türkiye’s compliance with its decision of 2014. The latter proposal was tabled by the Republic of Cyprus in September 2024.

In particular, the Republic of Cyprus proposed the adoption of a decision for the drafting of an interim resolution for referral of an interpretative question back to the Court, in relation to paragraph 63 of the ECtHR decision of 2014 on just satisfaction in the 4th interstate case Cyprus v. Türkiye.

A referral to the Court is regulated by Article 46.3 of the ECHR which envisages that the Committee of Ministers may refer a question to the Court to resolve an interpretative issue hindering the execution of a judgment. The Republic of Cyprus’ proposal was adopted by a majority vote, i.e. 25 member states voted in favour, 2 against and 19 abstained.

The potential activation of Article 46.3 of the ECHR in the upcoming DM meeting in June 2027 would be a benchmark for the Convention System, as it will be triggered for the very first time.

Finally, Türkiye failed to prevent the voting procedure on our proposal. More specifically, the Turkish delegation alleged that the adoption of a decision on the matter required 31 votes, a position that was rejected by member states. It then proceeded to ask for the postponement of the procedure until a written legal opinion was given on the aforementioned procedural matter, a position which was also rejected.

The Secretariat of the DM Committee will present its draft interpretative question in the upcoming meeting of June 2027.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs extends its appreciation and thanks to the Law Office of the Republic of Cyprus for its cooperation.

(AP/AS/MS)

articles.title: Press remarks by the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, following his meeting with the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Kyrgyz Republic (Kyrgyzstan), Mr Zheenbek Kulubaev, in Bishkek
articles.summary: It is indeed a great pleasure to be visiting Bishkek. It is the first time I am visiting your country, and this is just the second ever official visit of a
articles.content_html:
It is indeed a great pleasure to be visiting Bishkek. It is the first time I am visiting your country, and this is just the second ever official visit of a Minister of Foreign Affairs from Cyprus in 34 years of diplomatic relations.

I would like to express my sincere gratitude to the Minister for the warm hospitality he has extended to me and to my delegation.

This is the final stop of my tour in Central Asia, a region of strategic importance for the European Union (EU), but also for the Republic of Cyprus. And my visit here today is a clear sign of the Republic of Cyprus’ commitment to maintain close political dialogue and cooperation with Kyrgyzstan.

Before I move into the details of our meeting, I would also like to take the opportunity to congratulate the Minister for the election of Kyrgyzstan to the Non-Permanent Member position at the Security Council for the 2027–2028 term. This is indeed a great diplomatic and political achievement, a recognition of commitment to international law and to the principles underpinning the United Nations (UN) Charter. In this context, the Republic of Cyprus is looking forward to meaningful and close co-operation with Kyrgyzstan in upholding the UN Charter and public international law, especially during your term.

Today’s discussions provided an opportunity to review the current state of our bilateral relations and to explore avenues for enhancing cooperation in areas of mutual interest. We highlighted the importance of institutional continuity of contacts. To this end, we underlined the importance of also maintaining regular political dialogue and strengthening bilateral contacts at the technical and the political levels. In this regard, I extended an invitation to my counterpart to visit the Republic of Cyprus.

In terms of institutional framework, I particularly welcome today’s signing of the Memorandum of Understanding for Political Co-operation and the forthcoming signing in a few hours of the Agreement on the Avoidance of Double Taxation between our two countries. This, I believe, is a pivotal moment. It is an opportunity to create a momentum in terms of investment and trade, and it is an extremely important development in the history of our bilateral relations. It will contribute to establishing a more predictable, transparent legal and fiscal framework for both businesses and investment, and it could be the accelerator for economic growth.

Moreover, at the people-to-people level, Cyprus places particular emphasis on educational and academic cooperation. Scholarships, university partnerships and exchanges, and academic mobility have expanded steadily in recent years. In specific, the Republic of Cyprus has offered 46 scholarships to citizens of Kyrgyzstan in the period of three years. And we will continue creating these opportunities for studying in Cyprus to your citizens.

A significant part of our deliberations focused on relations between the EU and Kyrgyzstan. And as a matter of strategic direction, the Republic of Cyprus strongly supports enhanced cooperation between our two regions. We recognise that Central Asia has a growing strategic value, especially as regards connectivity, trade, transport diversification, and energy security.

We also exchanged views on the prospects for further cooperation under the EU-Kyrgyzstan framework. And this, of course, includes the enhanced partnership and co-operation agreement and also cooperation under the Generalised Scheme of Preferences Plus (GSP+) scheme. The EU is looking towards this region and its members. So does the Republic of Cyprus, with openness in dialogue, transparency in facilitative conditionality, and active engagement. The framework of cooperation should remain firmly grounded in the principles reflected in the Joint Declaration following the first EU-Central Asia Summit and the Joint Communique of the 20th EU-Central Asian Ministerial Meetings. These principles include, of course, full respect for international law, sovereignty, and territorial integrity of all states, and this is a commitment that we share.

Finally, in this light, I had the opportunity to brief my colleague about recent developments on the Cyprus problem. In conclusion, today’s discussion reaffirmed the value of maintaining open channels of communication in advancing practical cooperation in matters of neutral interests. Cyprus remains firmly committed to constructive engagement and dialogue with Kyrgyzstan, both bilaterally and also in the context of the broader framework of EU-Central Asia relations.

So, allow me, once again, in conclusion to thank you, dear Minister, for the constructive exchange of views, the spirit of openness and mutual respect that characterised our discussions today.

(AP/MS/AS)

articles.title: Address by the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, at the inauguration of the Embassy of the Republic of Cyprus in Astana
articles.summary: It is a great honour to be here today in Astana for the inauguration of the Embassy of the Republic of Cyprus to the Republic of Kazakhstan.
articles.content_html:
It is a great honour to be here today in Astana for the inauguration of the Embassy of the Republic of Cyprus to the Republic of Kazakhstan.

The decision to proceed with the opening of Diplomatic Missions is always the result of a complicated calculation. One that involves allocation of resources, assessment of the bilateral prospects and regional, strategic positioning. Above all, it is an issue about where we want to move towards. And, without a doubt, the outcome manifested here today is sending a clear political signal. Namely, for the Republic of Cyprus, Kazakhstan is a key partner in a region of great significance. A trade and investment collaborator. A political powerhouse with an influence that is bound to grow even further.

Together, and this I want to underscore, together we coordinated our steps, synchronised our will and proceeded with the reciprocal simultaneous opening of Diplomatic Missions.

In record time, the discussion started, advanced and matured into the effective implementation of decisions.

It is, therefore, accurate to state that today is a milestone in our bilateral relations.

Our Presidents have given the political direction, and all of us are now working to transition from the pathway to an avenue. An avenue of progress, for our peoples, for our countries.

Excellencies,

Ladies and Gentlemen,

The opening of this Embassy is not an endpoint. Rather, it is the beginning of a more ambitious, dynamic and forward-looking phase in Cyprus – Kazakhstan relations.

Our Ambassadors are the point of institutional reference. And I want to take the opportunity to extend my personal thanks to Ambassador of the Republic of Cyprus to Kazakhstan, Mr Petros Nacouzis, and to the Ambassador of Kazakhstan to Nicosia, Mr Nikolay Zhumakanov, for all the hard work and wish them every success.

We took the first steps. Now we need to pick up the pace. The political will and commitment are clear. And stronger than ever. Let us cease the opportunity.

Thank you.

(RM/MS/IA)

articles.title: The Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus hosted the Informal Meeting of EU Foreign
articles.summary: In the framework of the Cyprus Presidency of the Council of the European Union (EU), the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr
articles.content_html:
In the framework of the Cyprus Presidency of the Council of the European Union (EU), the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, together with the EU High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy, Ms Kaja Kallas, hosted today, 28 May 2026, the informal meeting of EU Ministers for Foreign Affairs (Gymnich) in Limassol, Cyprus.

The agenda of the informal meeting focused on two areas: developments in the Middle East and Russia’s war of aggression against Ukraine.

During the first session, the Ministers, with the participation of the Minister of External Affairs of the Republic of India, Mr Subrahmanyam Jaishankar, and the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, His Highness (HH) Prince Faisal bin Farhan al Saud, held in-depth discussions on the situation in the Middle East and wider regional developments. The discussion also covered the impact of current developments on maritime security, the importance of upholding international law, freedom of navigation, as well as the need to strengthen the resilience of supply chains.

During the second session, EU Foreign Ministers had an exchange on Russia’s ongoing war of aggression against Ukraine and the EU’s strategic positioning, including the way forward in maintaining momentum in support of Ukraine.

On the evening of Wednesday, 27 May, Minister Kombos hosted an informal dinner, which was also attended by the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Ukraine, Mr Andrii Sybiha, the Minister of External Affairs of the Republic of India, Mr Subrahmanyam Jaishankar, and HH Prince Faisal bin Farhad al Saud.

In the framework of the informal meeting, Minister Constantinos Kombos stated: “In today’s geopolitical environment, the challenges before us are significant. Today’s meeting reaffirmed that the EU remains united in its determination to protect its interests, to uphold its principles and to strengthen its role as a credible geopolitical actor.

Cyprus will continue to work in this direction, and to deliver and yield results until the last day of our Presidency”.

(AP/IA/MS)
articles.content_text:

articles.title: Statement by the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, ahead of today’s Informal Meeting of Foreign Ministers (Gymnich), in Limassol
articles.summary: Good morning from Limassol, from the port here. We are in Limassol, a maritime centre, a port city, a cosmopolitan port city at the crossroads of our three
articles.content_html:
Good morning from Limassol, from the port here. We are in Limassol, a maritime centre, a port city, a cosmopolitan port city at the crossroads of our three continents, and we are having today the Gymnich meeting.

In a while we will be welcoming our European colleagues and also our two distinguished guests, Prince Faisal from the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, and Dr Jaishankar, the Minister of Foreign Affairs of India.

Gymnich, as you know, is a very special meeting with a very long tradition for being an informal, open and frank discussion, with the aim of providing the strategic compass for the European Union (EU) foreign policy. This has been reflected in today’s agenda.

We are starting with a discussion about the situation, the state of play in the Middle East, and also as to the ways forward and the impact that the war is having on supply chains, and also how can we move forward in relation to the resilience of supply chains. Therefore, our two distinguished guests fit in exactly with the discussion that we are going to have. This has been, I remind you, a priority of the Presidency of Cyprus: maritime security, and the emphasis in the Middle East.

The second item of the agenda relates to our strategic positioning as regards to Ukraine, both in supporting Ukraine and also as regards how we can move forward. Again, this has been an issue that has been at the top of the priorities of the Cyprus Presidency. I can point to the approvement of the 90 billion loan, and also the 20th sanction package. So we’ll be having a strategic discussion about moving forward.

Finally, it’s European security strategy. We hope to be able to start a discussion today. This is exactly about how the EU positions itself in a very turbulent period moving forward, and this again has been part of our priorities. I can point out to the motto of our presidency, “A more autonomous Union, open to the world”.

I think it’s a very important opportunity we have here today to have this open and frank discussion. Our Presidency has faced a number of challenges, and we have adjusted, and we have managed to deliver. We will continue maximising our effectiveness and yielding results. Till the last day, the work will continue and will go on.

(AP/KA)

articles.title: The Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus hosts the Informal meeting of Foreign Affairs
articles.summary: The Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, will host the two-day Informal Meeting of Foreign Affairs Ministers
articles.content_html:
The Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, will host the two-day Informal Meeting of Foreign Affairs Ministers (Gymnich), in Limassol, on 27 and 28 May 2026, which he will co-chair with the High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy of the European Union (ΕU), Ms Kaja Kallas.

The Ministers of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of India, Mr Subrahmanyam Jaishankar, and of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, His Highness Prince Faisal Bin Farhan Bin Abdullah Al Saud, have also been invited and will attend the meeting.

On Wednesday, 27 May 2026, Minister Kombos will host a welcoming dinner for the Foreign Ministers, which will also be attended by the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Ukraine, Mr Andrii Sybiha.

On Thursday, 28 May 2026, the informal Gymnich meeting will take place at the Port of Limassol. During the meeting, the Foreign Ministers are expected to discuss current and regional issues, with the two distinguished guests from India and Saudi Arabia, and will subsequently discuss the war in Ukraine and the continuing Russian aggression.

Strategic initiatives on security and defence matters will also be discussed, including preparations for the new European Security Strategy.

(AP/AS/MS)
articles.content_text:
The Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, will host the two-day Informal Meeting of Foreign Affairs Ministers (Gymnich), in Limassol, on 27 and 28 May 2026, which he will co-chair with the High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy of the European Union (ΕU), Ms Kaja Kallas.
The Ministers of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of India, Mr Subrahmanyam Jaishankar, and of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, His Highness Prince Faisal Bin Farhan Bin Abdullah Al Saud, have also been invited and will attend the meeting.
On Wednesday, 27 May 2026, Minister Kombos will host a welcoming dinner for the Foreign Ministers, which will also be attended by the Minister of Foreign Affairs of Ukraine, Mr Andrii Sybiha.
On Thursday, 28 May 2026, the informal Gymnich meeting will take place at the Port of Limassol. During the meeting, the Foreign Ministers are expected to discuss current and regional issues, with the two distinguished guests from India and Saudi Arabia, and will subsequently discuss the war in Ukraine and the continuing Russian aggression.
Strategic initiatives on security and defence matters will also be discussed, including preparations for the new European Security Strategy.
(AP/AS/MS)

articles.title: Meeting of the Special Envoys for the Sahel in Brussels
articles.summary: The Meeting of the Special Envoys for the Sahel, co-hosted by the Cyprus Presidency of the Council of the European Union (EU) and the Office of the EU Special
articles.content_html:
The Meeting of the Special Envoys for the Sahel, co-hosted by the Cyprus Presidency of the Council of the European Union (EU) and the Office of the EU Special Representative for the Sahel region, Mr João Cravinho, took place on March 16–17, in Brussels.

The meeting brought together the Special Envoys for the Sahel of EU member states, like-minded countries, as well as regional and international partners.

High-level participants included the Special Representative of the United Nations (UN) Secretary-General and Head of the United Nations Office for West Africa and the Sahel (UNOWAS), Mr Leonardo Santos Simão, the African Union Special Representative and Head of the African Union Mission for Mali and the Sahel (MISAHEL), Dr Mamadou Tangara, and the Special Envoy of Ghana to the Alliance of Sahelian States, Colonel Larry Gbevlo-Lartey.

Over two days, participants exchanged views on the situation in the Sahel and examined ways for enhanced engagement in the region, including by working together more effectively to support local, sub-regional and regional initiatives.

(AP/LY/IA)

articles.title: Announcement by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus for Cypriot citizens in the Middle
articles.summary: Due to the regional developments in the wider Middle East, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus advises Cypriot citizens in the region to
articles.content_html:
Due to the regional developments in the wider Middle East, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus advises Cypriot citizens in the region to remain vigilant, closely monitor announcements by the local authorities, as well as information from international and local media, and take maximum protective measures.

Cypriot citizens in the region are also called to register on the online platform for Cypriots abroad Connect2CY, and to follow the social media accounts of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus and the Diplomatic Missions.

In case of emergency, Cypriot citizens are requested to contact the relevant Diplomatic Mission as follows:

(AP/GS/KA)

articles.title: Press Release by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs regarding the 2815/2026 UN Security Council Resolution on
articles.summary: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs welcomes the adoption of the 2815/2026 United Nations (UN) Security Council Resolution on the renewal of the mandate of the
articles.content_html:
The Ministry of Foreign Affairs welcomes the adoption of the 2815/2026 United Nations (UN) Security Council Resolution on the renewal of the mandate of the United Nation Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus (UNFICYP) for another 12 months, until January 31 2027. UNFICYP’s presence in Cyprus, based on its mandate, is indispensable for as long as the Turkish occupation continues. The Government of the Republic of Cyprus will continue to cooperate constructively with UNFICYP for the effective implementation of its mandate.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs welcomes the Security Council’s continued support to the UN Secretary-General’s efforts to restart negotiations aiming to achieve an enduring, comprehensive and just settlement of the Cyprus issue based on a bicommunal, bizonal federation with political equality, as set out in its relevant Resolutions.

With Resolution 2815/2026 the Security Council repeats, inter alia, its call for the reversal of all Turkish actions in the fenced-off city of Varosha and for the restoration of the status quo ante in Strovilia and the buffer zone.

In light of the continuing violations that are taking place in the buffer zone by the occupation forces, the significance of the Security Council’s call for reducing the tensions as a prerequisite for the creation of an appropriate climate and confidence building is underlined.

We look forward to returning to the negotiations the soonest with the aim to achieve a comprehensive settlement of the Cyprus issue in accordance with the relevant UN Security Council Resolutions.

(AP/MS/NZ)
articles.content_text:

articles.title: The Ambassador of Cyprus to China presents her credentials
articles.summary: The Ambassador of the Republic of Cyprus to the People’s Republic of China, Ms Koula Sophianou, presented her credentials to President Xi Jinping on 16
articles.content_html:
The Ambassador of the Republic of Cyprus to the People’s Republic of China, Ms Koula Sophianou, presented her credentials to President Xi Jinping on 16 January 2026, during an official ceremony held at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing, in which a total of 18 Ambassadors participated.

The presentation of credentials marks the formal commencement of the new Ambassador’s term in office at a moment of strategic significance, as 2026 marks the 55th anniversary of the establishment of diplomatic relations between the two countries.

Cyprus remains committed to strengthening dialogue with the People’s Republic of China on the basis of mutual respect and international law.

(AP/NZ/GS)

articles.title: Statement by the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Constantinos Kombos, following
articles.summary: First of all, we discussed the bilateral relations and the full scale of developments after last year’s summit where we strengthened our institutional
articles.content_html:
First of all, we discussed the bilateral relations and the full scale of developments after last year’s summit where we strengthened our institutional collaboration. The task now by our political leaders is about making sure that we have full implementation of what we have agreed on and also identifying new areas for cooperation. On this, our deliberations focused on practical measures moving forward and on a mechanism for monitoring the developments.

In addition, this trilateral relationship is based on solid ground. Our commitment to public international law and its manifestations, our rich history and of course our geographical proximity. These are extremely important principles, and they are extremely important especially in times of turbulence like the ones we are experiencing right now.

My two colleagues made reference to the various regional files that we discussed about, whether that’s Syria, Sudan, Yemen, Libya, Gaza, Iran. All this is happening near us, and our coordination and cooperation on how to respond to all these crises is extremely important, both as regards our bilateral relations, but also as regards our coordination in international fora.

In relation to Cyprus assuming the Presidency of the Council of the European Union (EU), the point for us, the priority, is to try and link the EU with this part of the world, with this region of ours. And as regards Egypt specifically, we want to build on the very positive outcomes of the recent EU-Egypt Summit, but also want to make sure that the new Pact for the Mediterranean includes Egypt in a way that actually reflects the importance and the value of Egypt for the European Union. Egypt has been a security gatekeeper for the European Union, not only in terms of migration. And we are strong supporters of Egypt in Brussels as regards receiving equitable support for all the work that it has been doing for the EU and the region in general.

In relation now to regional files, let me briefly make a comment to what has been happening recently in Gaza. And I want to remind Cyprus’ steadfast support for a two-state solution in relation to this matter. We have recognised the right of self-determination of Palestinian people back in 1988. We are welcoming the announcement about the second phase of President Trump’s Peace Plan and now it is about implementation and making sure that we have full endorsement by all interested parties for the principles that have been agreed.

In relation now to the situation in Iran, we are all very worried about what has been happening. The right of human life cannot be in any way underestimated. As a priority for us, we need to make sure that we also have de-escalation and we give diplomacy the chance, especially as regards issues relating to previous diplomatic negotiations.

About Syria, we are seeing worrying developments. Cyprus is a close neighbour to Syria, and we have been extending a hand of friendship towards Syria, only asking for good neighbourly relations. And good neighbourly relations should be about every neighbour and not selectively. We are also worried about the inclusion of minorities and their protection, and this is something on which, as Presidency of the Council of the EU, we will be focusing on.

Finally, I had the opportunity to brief my two friends about the Cyprus Problem and I want to publicly thank them, once again, for their principled and long-standing support towards the Republic of Cyprus, an approach based on public international law and the United Nations (UN) Security Council Resolutions.

In conclusion, this relationship is about predictability, reliability and consistency. And these are extremely rare commodities, rare and valuable, especially at times where we have shifting alliances and shifting positioning. It is on these basic principles that we have structured our enduring partnership, and it is on these principles that we continue moving forward.

Once again, dear Badr, thank you for your hospitality, and I look forward to the remaining of our deliberations next.

(AP/AS/LY)

articles.title: Press Release by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs regarding the UN Secretary-General’s Reports on his Mission
articles.summary: With regard to the UN Secretary-General’s Good Offices and Peacekeeping Force Reports to the UN Security Council, we note the following:
articles.content_html:
With regard to the UN Secretary-General’s Good Offices and Peacekeeping Force Reports to the UN Security Council, we note the following:

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus welcomes the Secretary-General’s reaffirmation of his commitment to continue his efforts to resume the negotiation process, with the goal of achieving a viable and comprehensive solution based on the agreed framework and the relevant United Nations (UN) Security Council Resolutions.

We share the Secretary-General’s contentment and importance given to the fact that substantial discussions over core aspects of the Cyprus issue have taken place for the first time in five years. True progress can only be achieved through such discussions for all core aspects of the Cyprus issue. We look forward to the implementation of the agreements reached and to a new meeting in extended format under the auspices of the UN Secretary General aiming to resume talks from where they were left off at Crans-Montana the soonest.

The Government fully shares the concern expressed again by the Secretary-General regarding the developments in the fenced area of Varosha, for which it is repeated that Türkiye is solely responsible and Ankara’s full compliance with Security Council Resolutions 550 (1984), 789 (1992) and 2771 (2025) is required.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs notes its concern regarding the fact that the recording, in the Report on UNFICYP, of violations by the occupying army, within and near the buffer zone and in its vicinity, especially in Deneia area, continues. At the same time, however, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs expresses its disappointment with the longstanding tendency of the UN to maintain an equidistant approach, resulting to the inaccurate and selective depiction of the realities on the ground as these are caused by the Turkish occupation and the methodology followed by the occupying forces, undermining the effort for the creation of a climate of trust.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs respects and considers of great importance the role that UNFICYP ought to have according to its mandate, to ensure normal living conditions, the respect and unhindered exercise of property rights and the development of civilian activities in the buffer zone. We look forward to the continuation of the close cooperation and coordination with UNFICYP to that effect, thus contributing in practice to the effort for trust-building among citizens.

Regarding the humanitarian mandate of the Peacekeeping Force, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs recalls the need for the Report to clearly record and highlight Türkiye’s responsibilities both in relation to the living conditions of the enclaved as well as on the issue of the missing persons.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs reaffirms its commitment to continued cooperation and close coordination with UNFICYP for the effective implementation of its mandate and for the ultimate goal of a just and viable solution to the Cyprus issue, based on the relevant UN Security Council Resolutions.

(AP/MS)

articles.title: Press Release by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus on the “Stella Souliotis” Human
articles.summary: The “Stella Souliotis” Human Rights Award was established in 2021 to pay tribute to the unique and multi-dimensional legacy of Ms Stella
articles.content_html:
The “Stella Souliotis” Human Rights Award was established in 2021 to pay tribute to the unique and multi-dimensional legacy of Ms Stella Souliotis, a remarkable figure in modern Cypriot history, who inspired successive generations of Cypriots. The annual award was established to honour a person or non-governmental organisation (NGO) for outstanding contribution in the field of human rights in the Republic of Cyprus.

The 2025 Award Assessment Committee, comprised of the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus (as Chair), the Minister of Justice and Public Order of the Republic of Cyprus and the Commissioner for Administration and Protection of Human Rights of the Republic of Cyprus as the three ex-officio members, as well as the two additional members for 2025, Dr Marios Cariolou and Mr Michael Vorkas, selected the Cyprus Third Age Observatory as the recipient of the “Stella Soulioti” Human Rights Award for 2025.

The Cyprus Third Age Observatory has demonstrated significant contribution to safeguarding the rights of older persons over the past ten years. Through targeted initiatives and dedicated actions, it provides essential support to older persons, particularly at a time when modern social and economic challenges are intensifying the difficulties they face.

The award ceremony will take place at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs on Wednesday, 17 December 2025.

(AP/IA/LY)
articles.content_text:
The “Stella Souliotis” Human Rights Award was established in 2021 to pay tribute to the unique and multi-dimensional legacy of Ms Stella Souliotis, a remarkable figure in modern Cypriot history, who inspired successive generations of Cypriots. The annual award was established to honour a person or non-governmental organisation (NGO) for outstanding contribution in the field of human rights in the Republic of Cyprus.
The 2025 Award Assessment Committee, comprised of the Minister of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus (as Chair), the Minister of Justice and Public Order of the Republic of Cyprus and the Commissioner for Administration and Protection of Human Rights of the Republic of Cyprus as the three ex-officio members, as well as the two additional members for 2025, Dr Marios Cariolou and Mr Michael Vorkas, selected the Cyprus Third Age Observatory as the recipient of the “Stella Soulioti” Human Rights Award for 2025.
The Cyprus Third Age Observatory has demonstrated significant contribution to safeguarding the rights of older persons over the past ten years. Through targeted initiatives and dedicated actions, it provides essential support to older persons, particularly at a time when modern social and economic challenges are intensifying the difficulties they face.
The award ceremony will take place at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs on Wednesday, 17 December 2025.
(AP/IA/LY)

articles.title: Press Release of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus on the decision of the Committee
articles.summary: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus expresses its satisfaction regarding today’s decision of the Committee of Deputy Ministers (Human
articles.content_html:
The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus expresses its satisfaction regarding today’s decision of the Committee of Deputy Ministers (Human Rights) of the Council of Europe (CoE) to maintain the supervision of the execution of the European Court of Human Rights’ (ECtHR) decision regarding the cluster of the property rights of displaced persons of the 4th interstate case Cyprus v. Türkiye.

The latter development is the outcome of systematic and targeted diplomatic efforts, carried out always in close coordination with the Legal Service of the Republic of Cyprus. It is noted that the support for Türkiye’s request for the closure of the supervision was decreased in comparison with last year’s voting procedure.

Cyprus’ long-standing position is that the illegal sale and exploitation of Greek Cypriot properties in the occupied areas by Türkiye, which has in recent years been intensified, violates the relevant ECtHR’s judgments.

Today’s development reaffirms Türkiye’s non-compliance with its obligation to terminate the ongoing violation of Greek-Cypriot displaced persons’ property rights. Simultaneously, it is clearly demonstrated that the termination of the supervision of the execution of the property cluster cannot be achieved while Türkiye fails to comply with relevant ECtHR judgments.

Furthermore, Cyprus proposed the adoption of a decision for the drafting an interim resolution for referral of an interpretative question back to the Court, in relation to paragraph 63 of the ECtHR decision of 2014 on Just Satisfaction in the 4th interstate case Cyprus v. Türkiye

A referral to Court is regulated by article 46.3 of the ECHR which envisages that the Committee of Ministers may refer a question to the Court to resolve a problem of interpretation hindering the execution of the judgment.

Cyprus’ proposal, which was presented before the Committee of Deputy Ministers for the first time last year, has gained positive momentum, showcasing an increase in support in comparison with last year’s voting.

(AP/GS/KA)

articles.title: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs offers two scholarships for the College of Europe
articles.summary: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs awards an annual scholarship to Cypriot citizens for studies at the College of Europe, on the basis of a relevant Memorandum
articles.content_html:
The Ministry of Foreign Affairs awards an annual scholarship to Cypriot citizens for studies at the College of Europe, on the basis of a relevant Memorandum of Understanding signed in 2021.

Beginning with the 2025-2026 academic year, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs will award two scholarships for study at the College.

The College of Europe was founded in 1949. Since 1992, it has operated across two university campuses in Bruges (Belgium) and Natolin (Poland), while in 2024 a new branch began operating in Tirana (Albania). It offers a wide range of postgraduate programmes focused on European Union (EU) affairs, providing postgraduate students with the necessary foundations to live and work elsewhere in the EU, as well as in an international environment more broadly.

In accordance with the provisions of the Memorandum, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs will award, for the 2026 – 2027 academic year, two scholarships to Cypriot citizens: one scholarship for study at the Bruges Campus amounting to €30,000 or at the Natolin Campus amounting to €29,000, and one scholarship for study at the Tirana Campus amounting to €27,000. The scholarships cover tuition fees and accommodation.

The deadline for submitting applications is 14 January 2026. Applications must be completed and submitted electronically through the College’s website.

It is noted that the initial selection of candidates is carried out by the College of Europe, after which candidates are assessed by the National Examination Committee of the Republic of Cyprus, which has been established jointly with the College of Europe.

For further information or clarification, interested individuals may contact the General Secretariat for European Affairs at: gsea@mfa.gov.cy.

(AP/AS/MS)

articles.title: Press release by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus on the anniversary of the illegal
articles.summary: Today marks 42 years since the illegal declaration of the secessionist entity in the areas of our country occupied by Türkiye. The violation of the
articles.content_html:
Today marks 42 years since the illegal declaration of the secessionist entity in the areas of our country occupied by Türkiye. The violation of the territorial integrity of the Republic of Cyprus, of international legality, of the principles of the Charter of the United Nations (UN), as well as of the relevant UN Security Council Resolutions, continues.

The Republic of Cyprus condemns the ongoing efforts by Türkiye and the occupying regime to promote the illegal entity and to advance a “two-state solution.” We call on all states to remain committed to the content of UN Security Council Resolutions 541 (1983) and 550 (1984), which unequivocally condemned this secessionist and legally void unilateral action. We will not accept the faits accomplis of the occupation, nor the legitimisation of the division of our homeland.

The international community, including the European Union (EU), has made it clear that it supports efforts to resolve the Cyprus issue only within the agreed framework, on the basis of a bizonal, bi-communal federation with political equality, as provided for in the relevant UN Security Council Resolutions. Within this framework, we look forward and are ready for the resumption of substantive negotiations for the resolution of the Cyprus issue.

We remain firmly committed to our duty to liberate and reunify Cyprus and its people, in accordance with international law, the UN Security Council Resolutions, the EU acquis, and the principles, values, and legislation of the EU.

(AP/KA/ΝΖ)

articles.title: Joint Statement between the United Arab Emirates and the Republic of Cyprus
articles.summary: The United Arab Emirates and the Republic of Cyprus reaffirm the depth of their strategic relations and their shared commitment to deepening and expanding
articles.content_html:
The United Arab Emirates and the Republic of Cyprus reaffirm the depth of their strategic relations and their shared commitment to deepening and expanding bilateral relations, as well as advancing regional stability, cooperation, and sustainable development. The meeting between Her Excellency Lana Nusseibeh, Minister of State, and His Excellency Dr. Constantinos Kombos, Minister of Foreign Affairs and Her Excellency Marilena Raouna, Deputy Minister for European Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus, reflected on the longstanding friendship between the two countries and their mutual desire to strengthen bilateral cooperation across humanitarian, economic, and multilateral fields.

During the course of the bilateral visit, Minister Nusseibeh called upon President Nikos Christodoulides to exchange on matters of mutual interest and convey the warm regards to His Highness Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, President of the United Arab Emirates.

Both sides reviewed areas of cooperation in light of Cyprus’s forthcoming Presidency of the Council of the European Union in 2026. They expressed readiness to deepen consultations on regional priorities, particularly those promoting coexistence, dialogue, and humanitarian diplomacy, consistent with their shared vision for a more peaceful and interconnected Mediterranean-Gulf region.

Minister Kombos updated on the latest developments on the Cyprus question, with Minister Nusseibeh reaffirming the UAE’s principled support for a solution in line with the agreed framework as set out in the relevant UNSC resolutions.

The two sides also reiterated their commitment to the UAE-Cyprus Comprehensive Strategic Partnership, formalised in February 2022, which encompasses cooperation across political consultations, defence and security, energy, trade and investment, tourism and culture, education, science and technology, and maritime transport. They underscored its importance as a framework for elevating bilateral relations to new strategic heights and agreed to launch the next phase of implementation – prioritising joint projects in clean energy, digital transformation, and maritime connectivity, alongside a dedicated coordination mechanism to track progress. The Ministers also highlighted the ongoing importance of people-to-people engagement and exchange.

During a visit to the Port of Limassol, both sides recognized the Amalthea Cyprus Maritime Corridor, established through close UAE-Cyprus coordination, as a complementary yet vital humanitarian lifeline for the Palestinian people in Gaza. They agreed to continue working closely with international and regional partners in line with the UN2720 coordinated mechanism to sustain and expand the corridor’s operations in support of the next phase of President Trump’s 20-Point Gaza Peace Plan.

The Ministers reaffirmed their shared commitment to achieving a comprehensive, just, and lasting peace based on the two-State solution, leading to the establishment of an independent Palestinian State, in accordance with relevant United Nations resolutions and international law.

On Sudan, the Ministers expressed alarm at the heinous attacks against civilians by RSF forces in El Fasher, as well as the escalation of conflict elsewhere in the country. They called on the RSF to ensure the protection of civilians and humanitarian personnel. The Ministers underscored that the primary responsibility for ending the conflict lies with both the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF). They reaffirmed the importance of immediate de-escalation, protection of civilians, and of rapid and unhindered humanitarian access throughout Sudan, and stressed the importance of ensuring accountability for crimes committed. The Ministers reaffirmed their support for Sudan’s sovereignty, unity, and territorial integrity. Cyprus welcomes the efforts of the Quad (United States, United Arab Emirates, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, and Egypt) and both sides agreed to remain actively engaged, in support of Quad and EU efforts to support an immediate ceasefire, facilitate humanitarian access, and advance an inclusive and transparent transition process that paves the way toward establishing an independent, civilian-led government with broad-based legitimacy and accountability.

The Ministers also discussed the war in Ukraine and reaffirmed their unwavering support for a comprehensive, just and lasting peace, based on respect for sovereignty, territorial integrity, and the principles of the United Nations Charter. The two sides emphasized the importance of humanitarian assistance and recognized both the UAE’s and Cyprus’s active roles in promoting dialogue and contributing to relief efforts. They noted the UAE’s facilitation of humanitarian assistance and prisoner exchanges between Ukraine and the Russian Federation, as well as Cyprus’s sustained support for Ukraine. The Ministers also discussed ways of cooperating on supporting Ukraine’s recovery.

The meeting further reviewed opportunities for cooperation in renewable energy, digital connectivity, maritime security, and humanitarian response, while reaffirming mutual support ahead of Cyprus’s EU Presidency and upcoming UAE-EU engagements. Both sides emphasized that the UAE-Cyprus partnership remains a model of constructive regional diplomacy grounded in international law and shared values of stability, development, and humanitarian responsibility.

(AP/AS/NZ)

articles.title: Travel Advice of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus for Tanzania
articles.summary: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus issued today Friday, 31 October 2025, travel advice to Cypriot citizens who are currently in or are
articles.content_html:
The Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Cyprus issued today Friday, 31 October 2025, travel advice to Cypriot citizens who are currently in or are planning to travel to Tanzania.

The Ministry of Foreign Affairs advises Cypriot citizens to exercise a high-degree of caution due to the ongoing unrest and the imposition of curfew in country.

Cypriot citizens currently in Tanzania are advised to avoid areas where there are gatherings and demonstrations, remain vigilant, and follow the instructions of local authorities. It is also recommended that they closely follow developments and information from international and local media, avoid non-essential movements and exercise maximum caution.

Cypriot citizens are also invited to register on the online registration platform for Cypriots abroad Connect2CY.

To see the Travel Advice, click here.

(AP/ΙΑ/MS)

articles.title: The Ambassador of the Republic of Cyprus to the Czech Republic presents her credentials
articles.summary: The new Ambassador of the Republic of Cyprus to the Czech Republic, Ms Aliki Pascali, presented her credentials to the President of the Czech Republic, Mr
articles.content_html:
The new Ambassador of the Republic of Cyprus to the Czech Republic, Ms Aliki Pascali, presented her credentials to the President of the Czech Republic, Mr Petr Pavel, on Wednesday, 8 October 2025, during an official ceremony at the Presidential Palace, in Prague.

This was followed by a private audience between Ambassador Pascali and President Pavel, during which they reviewed bilateral relations and discussed ways of further strengthening them.

Ms Pascali conveyed the warm regards of the President of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Nikos Christodoulides, which President Pavel reciprocated. She also conveyed an invitation from President Christodoulides to his Czech counterpart to pay an official visit to Cyprus.

The Ambassador subsequently had the opportunity to brief the Czech President on the Cyprus Question and on prospects for the resumption of negotiations aimed at finding a solution within the agreed framework. They also discussed matters relating to Cyprus’ role as a facilitator in providing security in the wider region of the Eastern Mediterranean and the Middle East, in light of recent developments, with particular reference to the “ESTIA” and “AMALTHEA” initiatives, for which President Pavel showed particular interest.

The Ambassador also informed Mr Pavel about the priorities of the Cyprus Presidency of the Council of the European Union during the first half of 2026.

(AP/MS)

articles.title: The Ambassador of the Republic of Cyprus to Iceland presents his credentials
articles.summary: The new Ambassador of the Republic of Cyprus to Iceland, with residence in Copenhagen, Mr Panayiotis Kyriacou, presented his credentials to the President of
articles.content_html:
The new Ambassador of the Republic of Cyprus to Iceland, with residence in Copenhagen, Mr Panayiotis Kyriacou, presented his credentials to the President of Iceland, Ms Halla Tómasdóttir, during an official ceremony held on 17 September 2025, at the Presidential Residence at Bessastaðir.

In the private audience that followed, the new Ambassador and the President exchanged views on bilateral issues. Mr Kyriacou conveyed to Ms Tómasdóttir the warm greetings of the President of the Republic of Cyprus, Mr Nikos Christodoulides, and extended an invitation to her to visit Cyprus. The President cordially accepted the invitation and conveyed her greetings to the President of the Republic of Cyprus.

(AP/MS/LY)

articles.title: Announcement of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs regarding the 5th annual “Stella Soulioti” Human
articles.summary: The Ministry of Foreign Affairs announces the 5th annual "Stella Soulioti" Human Rights Award and the opening of the nomination process on Friday, 19
articles.content_html:
The Ministry of Foreign Affairs announces the 5th annual “Stella Soulioti” Human Rights Award and the opening of the nomination process on Friday, 19 September 2025, for the submission of nominations or proposals for 2025. Anyone interested may either nominate themselves or put forward other individuals, groups, or organisations for the Award, by completing the appropriate form available through the links below.

The Award, established by a Decision of the Council of Ministers following a proposal by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, will be presented in December 2025 (exact date will be announced at a later stage), on the International Human Rights Day (which is annually celebrated on 10th December). It will be awarded to individuals/groups/organisations, which have consistently demonstrated outstanding work and activity, in a field or fields directly related to the promotion and protection of human rights.

Nominations or proposals may be submitted by or on behalf of individuals or groups of individuals legally residing in Cyprus, as well as by or on behalf of Non-Governmental Organizations that are registered and active in the Republic of Cyprus. Government authorities are not eligible for nomination and may not take part in the process. In addition, serving government officials and/or high-level public officials are excluded from nominating individuals, groups, or organisations for the Award.

In accordance with the relevant Decision of the Council of Ministers, the evaluation of nominations/ proposals and the selection of the award recipient will be made by a five-member Evaluation Committee comprising of the Minister of Foreign Affairs (Chairman), the Minister of Justice and Public Order, the Commissioner for Administration and Protection of Human Rights (Ombudsman), as well by two distinguished individuals with recognised experience in the promotion and protection of human rights.

The Award is accompanied by a prize of €10,000, which is intended to support the activities of the winning person/group/Organisation in the field of protection and promotion of human rights in Cyprus.

Application or nomination forms may be completed electronically and sent to humanrights@mfa.gov.cy or via post to “The Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Presidential Palace Avenue, 1447 Nicosia”, with the envelope clearly marked on the outside with the indication “Human Rights Award”.

For more information or clarification, you may call on (+357) 22651235 or send an email to humanrights@mfa.gov.cy.

The deadline for receipt of nominations/proposals expires on Monday, 10 November 2025, at 16:00 (Cyprus time zone).

(NZ/AS)
